user_request: Draft a commercial suit seeking damages for illegal termination of dealership agreement. Plaintiff invested substantial capital and developed territory market. Termination was arbitrary and contrary to agreement terms. Claim compensation for loss of profit, goodwill and unsold stock. Draft with proper breach of contract pleading.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks damages for illegal termination of a commercial dealership agreement. The plaintiff invested substantial capital and developed the territory market, but the termination was arbitrary and contrary to agreement terms. The claim includes compensation for loss of profit, goodwill, and unsold stock.

assumptions: Termination was without cause as per agreement terms
Jurisdiction lies in Civil Court based on contract value or location

201. Termination of agency .—An agency is terminated by the principal revoking his authority; or 
by the agent renouncing the business of the agency; or by the business of the agency being completed; or 
by either the principal or agent dying or becoming of unsound mind; or by the principal being adjudicated

absence of an express contract, be terminated to the prejudice of such interest.

legal_research.summary:
[Statute of limitations in contract claims : an overview - iPleaders] Under normal circumstances, the Indian Limitation Act of 1963, under Section 14 of the Limitation Act, provides that a claim for breach of contract must be made within three years from the date of such breach .
[Applicabilty of Limitation Act to Indian Contract Act] In other words, if there is no specific provision in the Limitation Act or any other law that prescribes a limitation period for a particular type of suit, then the plaintiff can file such a suit within three years from the date when the right ...
[The Period of Limitation For A Suit For Compensation For The Breach of Any Contract | PDF | Breach Of Contract | Damages] It is three years from the date the contract was broken, or from the date of the most recent breach if there were successive breaches, or from the date the continuing breach ceased .
[1 Limitation Periods: Overview (India)] Limitation Act prescribes separate periods for the preference of suit in tort, and for breach of · obligations under contract.
[Dhapai vs Dalla And Ors. on 2 April, 1969] Three years When the contract is broken, or (where there are successive breaches) when the breach in respect of which the suit is instituted occurs or (where the breach is continuing) when it ceases . 120. Suit for which no period of limitation is provided elsewhere in this Schedule.
[The Plaint: Foundational Document in Civil Litigation under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 - Bhatt & Joshi Associates] Order IV, Rule 1 of the CPC mandates that “ Every suit shall be instituted by presenting a plaint in duplicate to the Court or such officer as it appoints in this behalf ” and that “Every plaint shall comply with the rules contained in Orders ...
[Strict Compliance with Section 80 of the Civil Procedure Code for Suits Against the Government: Amar Nath Dogra v. Union of India: Supreme Court Of India | CaseMine] During the tenure of his license, Dogra alleged breaches of contractual stipulations by the Excise Authorities, leading him to serve a notice under Section 80 of the Civil Procedure Code (CPC) and subsequently file a suit claiming damages.
[Revisiting The Principles Of Damages Under The Contract Regime In India - Contracts and Commercial Law - India] The Supreme Court in M.N. Gangappa v. Atmakur Nagabhushanam Shetty & Co. and Anr., (AIR 1972 SC 696) has observed that in absence of a strict procedure for calculation of damages arising out of breach of contract, damages would be calculated based on the facts and circumstances of each case.
[Documents Filed with Plaint: Comprehensive Legal Framework and Judicial Interpretation - Bhatt & Joshi Associates] The judgment highlighted that allowing parties to introduce documents at later stages without proper justification would undermine the procedural integrity of civil suits and potentially prejudice the opposing party’s ability to prepare an adequate defense. The decision also clarified that the requirement to file documents with the plaint is not merely directory but mandatory, meaning that non-compliance carries substantive consequences.

The plaintiff will make all statements, which is the first step in the ...
[The basic court procedure for filing a civil suit in India | SoOLEGAL] File requisite amount of procedure - fee in the court. File 2 copies of plaint for each defendant in the court . Of, the 2 copies for each defendant, one shall be sent by Register/post/courier, and one by Ordinary post.
[Strict Compliance with Section 80 of the Civil Procedure Code for Suits Against the Government: Amar Nath Dogra v. Union of India: Supreme Court Of India | CaseMine] The Supreme Court examined whether Amar Nath Dogra’s suit against the Union of India complied with the procedural requirements of Section 80 CPC , which mandates that a notice must be served before initiating a lawsuit against the government.
[The Plaint: Foundational Document in Civil Litigation under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 - Bhatt & Joshi Associates] The plaint thus functions as both a procedural requirement and a substantive pleading that shapes the contours of litigation from inception to resolution. Section 26 of the CPC establishes the fundamental principle governing the institution of civil suits, providing that “Every suit shall be instituted by the presentation of a plaint or in such other manner as may be prescribed.” This provision underscores the mandatory nature of the plaint in civil litigation, making it an indispensable prerequisite for the valid institution of any civil suit in Indian courts.

IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}} AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. __________ / 20____\n\n{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, Aged about {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, Residing at: {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}.                                                        ... DEFENDANT\n\nSUIT FOR DAMAGES FOR BREACH OF COMMERCIAL DEALERSHIP AGREEMENT OF Rs. {{DAMAGES_AMOUNT}}/- WITH INTEREST AND COSTS UNDER SECTION 26 OF CPC, 1908 READ WITH {{PRIMARY_PROVISION}}

Plaintiff: {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged about {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, Residing at: {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}.\nDefendant: {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged about {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, Residing at: {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}.


JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued.\n2. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try the present suit as the cause of action arose within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Court and the party and subject matter fall within its jurisdiction. This Court has pecuniary jurisdiction as the suit claim falls within the pecuniary limits prescribed. This Court has subject matter jurisdiction to entertain suits of this nature.


FACTS OF THE CASE

2. The Plaintiff entered into a dealership arrangement with the Defendant on {{DEALERSHIP_DATE}} and has developed the territory market in reliance on the said agreement.\n4. The Plaintiff has invested substantial capital and incurred expenses for setting up, marketing, inventory, and operational costs amounting to Rs. {{INVESTMENT_AMOUNT}} and has built up goodwill in the territory.\n5. On {{TERMINATION_DATE}}, the Defendant terminated the dealership agreement, which termination was arbitrary, unwarranted and contrary to the express terms of the agreement, without just cause or due process and in breach of the contractual obligations incumbent on the Defendant.\n6. The Plaintiff served a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} through registered post A.D., calling upon the Defendant to {{NOTICE_DEMAND}} within 15 days of receipt of the said notice (a true copy of the legal notice along with the postal receipt and acknowledgment card is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure C).\n7. Despite receipt of the legal notice, the Defendant has refused to reinstate the dealership or compensate the Plaintiff for the breach, resulting in loss of profits, goodwill and unsold stock, and the Plaintiff is therefore entitled to damages.\n8. The breach is continuing and damages are recoverable for the period from the date of termination till date of realization of the suit amount.


LEGAL BASIS

3. The Defendant is liable for breach of contract by terminating the dealership arrangement contrary to its express terms, entitling the Plaintiff to damages for loss of profits, goodwill, and unsold stock.\n9. In the alternative, the Plaintiff relies on general contract principles and the terms of the dealership agreement to claim damages for breach, which are recoverable in a suit for breach of contract before this Hon'ble Court.


CAUSE OF ACTION

4. The cause of action first arose on the date of termination, {{TERMINATION_DATE}}, and is a continuing cause of action which subsists and continues till realization of the suit amount.


LIMITATION

5. The suit is within the period of limitation under Article 113 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which prescribes a period of 3 years from the date when the cause of action accrues. The breach occurred on {{TERMINATION_DATE}}, and the suit is filed within the prescribed period.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

6. The suit is valued at Rs. {{DAMAGES_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{DAMAGES_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. The requisite court fee of Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}/- has been paid on this plaint.


INTEREST

7. Pre-suit interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from {{TERMINATION_DATE}} till the date of filing of this suit;\n14. Pendente lite interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum or at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just and reasonable during the pendency of this suit under Section 34 of the CPC, 1908;\n15. Future interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree till actual realization.


PRAYER

8. In the premises stated hereinabove, it is most respectfully prayed that this Hon'ble Court may be pleased to:\n   (a) Pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for Rs. {{TOTAL_DAMAGES}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_WORDS}} Only) together with interest as claimed hereinabove;\n   (b) Award pre-suit interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from {{TERMINATION_DATE}} till the date of filing;\n   (c) Award pendente lite interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just and reasonable;\n   (d) Award future interest from the date of decree till the date of actual realization;\n   (e) Award costs of this suit including pre-litigation costs incurred for issuing legal notice;\n   (f) Grant such other and further relief as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.


LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Sr. No.  Description  Annexure\n1. Copy of dealership agreement and addenda  Annexure A\n2. Evidence of investment and market development costs  Annexure B\n3. Legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} with postal receipt  Annexure C


VERIFICATION

9. I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 22 of this plaint are true to my personal knowledge and belief, that no part of the cause of action has arisen in any other court, and that I have not concealed any material facts. Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.\n\n                                                        {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}\n                                                        PLAINTIFF\n\nThrough:\n{{ADVOCATE_NAME}}\nAdvocate\nEnrollment No. {{ADVOCATE_ENROLLMENT}}\n{{ADVOCATE_ADDRESS}}
